definition: How far you can move a joint using only your own muscles — no help from your hands, gravity, or a partner. Increasing your active range matters more for daily life than your passive range.
The seven vertebrae in your neck (C1-C7). When you read 'neck stretch', it's the cervical spine being targeted.
An exercise where the working hand or foot is fixed against a surface (push-up, squat). Generally safer for joints than open-chain variants because the joint can't 'escape' a bad position.
Lengthening a muscle while it's under tension — like the lowering part of a squat. Eccentric work is the most efficient way to build flexibility and tendon resilience.
A group of three long muscles running parallel to your spine. They straighten your back when you stand up and resist forward folding. Tight erectors are a common cause of low-back stiffness.
Connective tissue that wraps every muscle. It's not as elastic as muscle itself, which is why stretches that feel 'thick' or 'rubbery' often need long holds (60-90s) to release.
Bending a joint to decrease its angle — curling your spine into a Cat Pose, bringing your knee toward your chest. The opposite of extension.
A group of muscles at the front of your hip (mainly psoas and iliacus) that lift your knee. Sitting all day keeps them short, which then pulls your low back forward.
The top edge of your pelvis — the hard bony ridge you feel when you put your hands on your hips. Many stretches reference 'square the hips' relative to this landmark.
The five vertebrae of your lower back (L1-L5). The vertebrae here are the largest and bear the most load — and consequently are where most stretching apps focus.
Flexibility with control. A person with great mobility can not only reach a deep stretch but actively hold and move in and out of it. Mobility-not-flexibility is the goal for injury prevention.
The natural S-curve of your spine when standing relaxed — not rounded, not arched. Most strength work and many stretches start from neutral.
Rotation of the pelvis around the hip joints. Anterior tilt (forward) hollows the low back; posterior tilt (backward) flattens it. Learning to find both is a core skill for back health.

Proprioceptive Neuromuscular Facilitation. A stretch technique where you contract the muscle being stretched for 5-10 seconds, then relax and stretch deeper. Effective but advanced — don't start here.
A deep hip flexor that runs from the lumbar spine to the inner thigh. Tightness here pulls the lower back into anterior tilt and is implicated in many back-pain cases.
The joint where your sacrum (base of the spine) meets the iliac bones (pelvis). Often called 'SI joint'. Limited motion, but when it gets stuck or inflamed it generates a lot of low-back pain.
Holding a stretch in one position for 20-60 seconds. Most of the stretches in this app are static. Effective for flexibility; not what you want as a pre-workout warmup (use dynamic instead).
The twelve vertebrae of your mid-back (T1-T12). Designed for rotation. Stiffness here is what makes 'check your blind spot' uncomfortable.
